summary: Jeff Atwood advises readers against buying the first-generation iPhone despite being a fan of Apple's entry into the mobile market. Drawing from his experience with a Samsung Blackjack smartphone, he argues that the iPhone's lack of 3G connectivity is a critical flaw that will make internet browsing painfully slow over EDGE connections. He dismisses Apple's suggestion to rely on WiFi as unrealistic, noting that free public WiFi is increasingly rare and expensive. While acknowledging the iPhone's revolutionary potential, Atwood predicts Apple will release a significantly improved second-generation model within 12 months and urges patience.
key_points: The mobile phone market is a 'sad, pathetic wasteland' that desperately needs Apple's competitive pressure to stop making user-hostile products
Having mobile internet access is transformative - like having the sum of the world's knowledge in your palm, anywhere
3G provides near cable-modem speeds while EDGE is painfully slow (around 10 kb/sec), making the iPhone's EDGE-only connectivity a serious limitation
Reliance on WiFi is not a viable alternative since free public WiFi hotspots are increasingly rare and locked down
Based on Apple's iPod iteration cycle, a significantly improved iPhone 2.0 with 3G is likely within 12 months
money_quotes: The mobile phone market is a sad, pathetic wasteland in desperate need of improvement.
It's like having the sum of the entire world's knowledge in the palm of your hand, wherever you happen to be.
3G is darn near a cable modem experience.
EDGE data connections are unbelievably painful.
If you think reliance on WiFi is an advantage of the iPhone, check your reality distortion field.
The 3G cellular data network frees me from dependency on expensive, unreliable, hard to find WiFi hotspots.
Never, never has it been more true for any technology product: wait for version 2.0 before buying.
Not all buying decisions have to be rational.
key_insight: Revolutionary hardware with crippled connectivity is a poor value proposition when history suggests a dramatically improved version will arrive within a year.
